Authors & Year: Ali, A.; Abd Razak, S.; Al-Dhaqm, A.; Nasser, M.; Othman, S.H.; Eisa, T.A.E.; Elhassan, T.; Elshafie, H.; Saif, A. (2022)
Title: Financial Fraud Detection Based on Machine Learning: A Systematic Literature Review
Paper Summary: This paper presents a systematic literature review (SLR) of machine-learning-based approaches for financial fraud detection. Using the Kitchenham SLR methodology, the authors reviewed studies published between 2010 and 2021. After applying inclusion, exclusion, and quality assessment criteria, 93 peer-reviewed studies were analyzed. The review identifies the most common fraud categories, datasets, machine learning algorithms, and evaluation metrics used in financial fraud detection. The study concludes that Support Vector Machine (SVM) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) are the most widely used algorithms, while credit card fraud is the most frequently studied fraud type. The paper highlights existing research gaps and proposes directions for future work.
Algorithms used: Support Vector Machine (SVM), Artificial Neural Network (ANN), Hidden Markov Model (HMM), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Decision Tree (DT), Random Forest (RF), Logistic Regression, Naïve Bayes, Fuzzy Logic, Genetic Algorithm, Deep Learning

ROC-AUC
Key Innovation:
Comprehensive SLR covering multiple financial fraud domains, not limited to credit card fraud
Systematic comparison of ML algorithms, datasets, fraud types, and evaluation metrics
Identification of research gaps related to datasets, imbalance issues, and real-world deployment
Main Finding: Machine learning techniques are effective for financial fraud detection, with SVM and ANN being the most popular approaches. Credit card fraud dominates the literature, while other fraud domains such as insurance, healthcare, and financial statement fraud remain underexplored.

Authors & Year: Hafez, I. Y., Hafez, A. Y., Saleh, A., Abd El-Mageed, A. A., & Abohany, A. A. (2025)
Title: A systematic review of AI-enhanced techniques in credit card fraud detection
Paper Summary: This paper presents a systematic review of AI-enhanced techniques for credit card fraud detection, focusing on machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), and meta-heuristic optimization (MHO) approaches. It analyzes more than thirty studies published between 2019 and 2024, comparing methodologies, datasets, algorithms, and performance metrics. The study highlights strengths, weaknesses, and limitations of existing approaches and emphasizes the need for continuously adaptive AI models to address evolving fraud patterns.

Authors & Year: Himangshu Paul, Alexander Nikolaev (2021)
Publisher: Springer Science + Business Media LLC (Springer Nature)
Title: Fake review detection on online E-commerce platforms: a systematic literature review
Paper Summary: This paper provides a systematic literature review (SLR) of Fake Review Detection (FRD) on online e-commerce platforms. It analyzes the evolution of research approaches used to detect deceptive and manipulated online reviews. The authors categorize fake review detection methods based on different types of “footprints” left by spammers (content, writing style, behavior, networks, ratings, and collusion). The paper also discusses why current approaches and regulations have had limited success and proposes future directions, including decentralized and user-empowered review systems.
Algorithms used: Logistic Regression, Naïve Bayes, Support Vector Machine (SVM), Decision Trees, Random Forest, Boosting methods, Topic Models (LDA, hierarchical LDA), Neural Networks (RNN, CNN), Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), Co-training, Positive and Unlabeled (PU) Learning, EM Algorithm
Best Algorithm: No single best algorithm is identified due to domain variability and dataset dependency.
Key Innovation:
Comprehensive and systematic classification of fake review detection approaches
Chronological evolution of FRD techniques
Distinction between fake-review detection and fake-reviewer detection
Emphasis on behavioral and collusion-based spamming
Identification of gaps and limitations in current FRD research
Main Finding: Despite extensive research, fake review detection remains an unsolved problem due to lack of ground truth, adaptive spammers, and increasingly sophisticated fake review generation methods.
Study limitations:
No experimental validation
Relies on existing published studies
No unified benchmark or dataset
Cannot quantify real-world effectiveness of reviewed approaches
Future work proposed:
Decentralized and user-empowered review systems
Trust-based review aggregation

Authors & Year: Reena More, Pankaj Dashore (2025)
Title: Securing Digital Transactions: An Enhanced Deep Learning Approach for Online Payment Fraud Detection
Paper Summary: This paper presents a comprehensive systematic review of machine learning and deep learning techniques for online payment fraud detection and proposes a novel transformer-enhanced deep learning methodology. It analyzes supervised, unsupervised, semi-supervised, and hybrid fraud detection models, highlighting their strengths and limitations. The study addresses major challenges such as data imbalance, scalability, real-time detection, evolving fraud patterns, interpretability, and privacy. A Transformer-Enhanced CNN combined with HRNN and optimized using Differential Evolution is proposed to improve accuracy, adaptability, and real-time performance in online payment fraud detection systems.

Year: 2023
Publisher: ASRC Procedia: Global Perspectives in Science and Scholarship
Title: Machine Learning for Fraud Detection in Digital Banking: A Systematic Literature Review
Paper Summary: This paper presents a systematic literature review of 118 peer-reviewed studies and institutional reports examining the application of machine learning techniques for fraud detection in digital banking. Following PRISMA guidelines, the study synthesizes supervised, unsupervised, deep learning, and hybrid approaches used to detect fraud in highly imbalanced financial transaction datasets. The review highlights the dominance of supervised learning due to interpretability, the growing importance of anomaly detection for novel fraud patterns, and the emergence of deep learning models (RNNs, CNNs, GNNs) for sequential and graph-based transaction analysis. It also emphasizes the role of evaluation metrics (precision, recall, F1-score, PR-AUC) and explainability tools (SHAP, LIME) in regulated banking environments. Cross-regional analysis reveals differences in regulatory influence across the EU, North America, and emerging economies. Persistent challenges include data imbalance, interpretability, real-time deployment constraints, reproducibility, and robustness under concept drift.
Algorithms used:
Supervised: Decision Trees, Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machines (SVM), Random Forest, Gradient Boosting
Unsupervised: K-Means, DBSCAN, Isolation-based anomaly detection, Gaussian,Mixture Models, Self-Organizing Maps
Deep Learning: Artificial Neural Networks, RNN, LSTM, GRU, CNN, Autoencoders, Variational Autoencoders (VAE), Graph Neural Networks (GNN)
Performance evaluation:
Precision
Recall
F1-score
ROC-AUC
PR-AUC
Cost-sensitive evaluation
Best Algorithm:
Supervised models are most widely adopted

Strong focus on operational deployment and governance
Main Finding:
Supervised learning remains dominant due to interpretability
Unsupervised methods are essential for novel fraud detection
Deep learning excels in sequential and graph-based transaction modeling
Hybrid approaches provide the best balance of accuracy and adaptability
Study limitations:
No experimental implementation
Dependence on secondary studies
Lack of standardized datasets and benchmarks
Limited reproducibility due to proprietary data
Future work proposed:
Improved reproducibility and open benchmarks
Robustness to concept drift and adversarial fraud
Deeper integration of explainability with governance
Greater use of hybrid and graph-based models
Stronger theoretical grounding (criminology + ML)

Paper Summary: This paper presents a systematic literature review of 51 peer-reviewed studies published between 2018 and 2023 that apply machine learning techniques to financial fraud detection. The study highlights Random Forest as the most commonly used supervised algorithm, emphasizes challenges such as class imbalance, data transparency, and model interpretability, and outlines future research directions including hybrid models, reinforcement learning, and standardized evaluation metrics.

Authors & Year: Elena-Adriana Minastireanu, Gabriela Meșniță — 2019
Title: An Analysis of the Most Used Machine Learning Algorithms for Online Fraud Detection
Paper Summary: This paper presents a systematic quantitative literature review of machine-learning algorithms used for online fraud detection, with a focus on banking and online transactions. The authors analyze peer-reviewed journal and conference papers published between 2010–2019, classifying algorithms based on three key criteria: accuracy, fraud coverage, and cost efficiency. A hierarchical typology of supervised and unsupervised learning techniques is proposed. The study concludes that supervised learning algorithms, particularly SVM, ANN, and Decision Tree, provide the best performance for online fraud detection, albeit at higher computational costs.

Authors & Year: Badr Omair; Ahmad Alturki (2020)
Publisher: IEEE Access (Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers – IEEE)
Title: A Systematic Literature Review of Fraud Detection Metrics in Business Processes
Paper Summary: This paper presents a systematic literature review focusing on fraud detection metrics within business processes, specifically process-based fraud (PBF), which involves deviations from standard operating procedures. Using qualitative content analysis and PRISMA-like methodology, the authors analyze 75–76 studies to assess existing fraud detection metrics and their coverage of business process perspectives. The study identifies major gaps in current metrics, particularly their insufficient theoretical validation and limited coverage of business process components, perspectives, and presentation layers. The paper proposes a conceptual foundation for improving PBF detection metrics.
Algorithms used:
Process mining algorithms
Association Rule Learning (ARL)
Fuzzy Logic
Classification models
Clustering
Regression
Outlier detection
Rule-based expert systems
Performance evaluation:
Accuracy
Precision
Recall
False alarm rate
Fraud detection effectiveness (qualitative comparison)
Key Innovation:
First systematic literature review dedicated specifically to fraud detection metrics in business processes
Identification of conceptual gaps in existing PBF detection metrics
Integration of BPM perspectives, process ingredients, auditing needs, and presentation layers into fraud detection analysis
Main Finding: Although many fraud detection metrics exist (e.g., red flags), very few focus explicitly on process-based fraud. Existing PBF metrics are limited in number, weakly validated, and fail to cover essential business process perspectives. Effective fraud detection in organizations requires tighter integration between fraud risk management and business process management.
Study limitations:
No empirical validation or real-world case study
Reliance on secondary literature only
Lack of quantitative comparison of metrics
Absence of real-time or automated implementation
Future work proposed:
Extend existing PBF detection metrics
Empirically validate metrics in real business contexts
Conduct case studies in specific industries
Integrate metrics into automated fraud detection systems
Evaluate effectiveness of metrics against common fraud risks

Publisher:
Systematic Literature Review Structure

1. Title

Clearly state the topic and scope of the review.

2. Abstract

A summary of the review, including the objective, methods, results, and conclusions.

3. Introduction

Background: Introduce the topic and explain its significance.
Objective: Clearly state the research question(s) or hypothesis.
Scope: Define the boundaries of the review.

4. Methodology

Search Strategy:
Databases searched (e.g., PubMed, Scopus).
Keywords and search terms.
Time frame of the literature included.
Inclusion and Exclusion Criteria:
Define the criteria for including or excluding studies.
Data Extraction:
Methods for extracting data (e.g., a standardized form).
Quality Assessment:
Tools used to assess the quality of the included studies (e.g., Cochrane risk-of-bias tool).
Data Synthesis:
Describe how data will be analyzed (qualitative or quantitative synthesis).

5. Results

Study Selection:
Flowchart (e.g., PRISMA flow diagram) showing the selection process.
Study Characteristics:
Summary table of included studies (authors, year, design, population, findings).
Quality Assessment Results:
Summary of the quality assessment of the studies assessed.

6. Discussion

Summary of Findings:
A narrative synthesis of key findings.
Comparison with Other Reviews:
Discuss how your findings relate to existing literature.
Implications:
Theoretical and practical implications of the findings.
Limitations:
Discuss any limitations of the review process and included studies.


7. Conclusion

Recap the major findings and their relevance.
Provide recommendations for future research.

8. References

List all sources cited, formatted according to a specific citation style (e.g., APA, MLA).

9. Appendices (if necessary)

Additional material that supports the review, such as search strategies or additional tables.
Notes

PRISMA Guidelines: Consider following the PRISMA guidelines for systematic reviews and meta-analyses, which provide a clear checklist for reporting.
Collaboration: It’s beneficial to involve co-authors in the review process for comprehensive coverage and insight.
This structured approach will help ensure that your systematic literature review is comprehensive and methodologically sound.
